Allocate largest subnet first
Minimises IP address waste

sumCards.label: VLSM Process
sumCards.content:
1) Sort needs: large → small
2) Allocate largest first
3) Continue from remaining space

qa.q: What is supernetting and why is it used?
qa.a: Supernetting (route aggregation) combines multiple smaller networks into a single larger network by using a shorter prefix. Example: instead of advertising 8 separate /24 routes to the internet, advertise one /21 that covers all of them. It is used to: reduce routing table sizes (routers store fewer routes), speed up routing decisions, reduce routing update traffic, and create a more stable network.

qa.q: Aggregate the following two networks: 172.16.0.0/24 and 172.16.1.0/24. Show your working.
qa.a:
Convert to binary:
172.16.0.0 = 10101100.00010000.00000000.00000000
172.16.1.0 = 10101100.00010000.00000001.00000000
Compare bit by bit from left: the first 23 bits match (they differ at bit 24 — the last bit of the 3rd octet). Common prefix = /23.
Supernet: 172.16.0.0/23. This covers 172.16.0.0–172.16.1.255 (512 addresses), which includes both /24 networks.

qa.q: What is VLSM and how does it improve on fixed-length subnetting?
qa.a: VLSM (Variable Length Subnet Masking) allows using different prefix lengths within the same address block — creating subnets of exactly the right size for each requirement. Fixed-length subnetting creates all subnets the same size — if you need 3 hosts, you still get a /24 (254 hosts), wasting 251 addresses. VLSM allocates a /30 (2 hosts) instead, saving the rest for other subnets.

qa.q: Using VLSM, allocate subnets from 10.0.0.0/24 for: 60 hosts, 25 hosts, and a router link (2 hosts).
qa.a:
Sort largest to smallest: 60, 25, 2.
60 hosts: need 62 → 2^6=64 → /26. Subnet: 10.0.0.0/26. Range: .1–.62. Broadcast: .63.
25 hosts: need 27 → 2^5=32 → /27. Subnet: 10.0.0.64/27. Range: .65–.94. Broadcast: .95.
Router link: need 2 → /30. Subnet: 10.0.0.96/30. Range: .97–.98. Broadcast: .99.
All requirements met from one /24 with minimal waste.

qa.q: What is the difference between subnetting and supernetting?
qa.a: Subnetting divides a large network into smaller subnets by making the prefix longer (adding bits). Example: /24 → /26 (creates 4 smaller subnets). Used to organise networks and reduce broadcast domains. Supernetting combines multiple smaller networks into a larger one by making the prefix shorter (removing bits). Example: four /26 networks → /24. Used to reduce routing table size by advertising one route instead of many.

topics.subtitle: 9.1 HTTP & HTTPS · 9.2 Email Protocols · 9.3 DNS & DHCP · 9.4 File Transfer & Remote Access

notes.title: HTTP and HTTPS
notes.body:
HTTP (HyperText Transfer Protocol): the foundation of data communication on the World Wide Web. Stateless, text-based, client-server protocol. Client sends request; server sends response.

HTTP Methods:
  • GET: retrieve a resource (no body)
  • POST: submit data to create a resource
  • PUT: replace/update a resource
  • DELETE: remove a resource
  • PATCH: partially update a resource
HTTP Status Codes:
  • 200 OK · 201 Created · 204 No Content
  • 301 Moved Permanently · 302 Found (redirect)
  • 400 Bad Request · 401 Unauthorised · 403 Forbidden · 404 Not Found
  • 500 Internal Server Error · 503 Service Unavailable
HTTPS: HTTP + TLS/SSL encryption. All data encrypted — prevents eavesdropping and tampering. The padlock in your browser. Uses port 443 (HTTP uses port 80). Modern browsers flag HTTP sites as 'Not Secure'.

notes.title: Email Protocols: SMTP, POP3, IMAP
notes.body:
SMTP (Simple Mail Transfer Protocol): used to send email from client to server, and between mail servers. Port 25 (server-to-server), 587 (client submission with auth). Push protocol.

POP3 (Post Office Protocol v3): used to retrieve email from server to client. Port 110 (unencrypted), 995 (SSL). Downloads emails and typically deletes them from server. No sync between devices.

IMAP (Internet Message Access Protocol): used to access email on server without downloading. Port 143 (unencrypted), 993 (SSL). Emails stay on server — synced across all devices. Better for multi-device access.

ProtocolPurposePortStores on
SMTPSend email25 / 587—
POP3Download email110 / 995Client
IMAPAccess email143 / 993Server

notes.title: DNS and DHCP in Detail
notes.body:
DNS (Domain Name System): the internet's phone book — translates domain names to IP addresses.
DNS Record Types:
  • A: maps domain to IPv4 address (www → 1.2.3.4)
  • AAAA: maps domain to IPv6 address
  • CNAME: alias — maps one name to another (mail.company.com → company.com)
  • MX: mail exchanger — directs email to the correct mail server
  • NS: nameserver — identifies authoritative DNS servers for the domain
  • TXT: text records — used for SPF (email validation), domain verification
  • PTR: reverse DNS — maps IP address back to domain name
DNS uses port 53 (UDP for queries, TCP for large responses/zone transfers).

DHCP (Dynamic Host Configuration Protocol): automatically assigns network configuration to devices.
DORA Process:
  1. Discover: client broadcasts 'I need an IP'
  2. Offer: server offers an IP address
  3. Request: client accepts the offered IP
  4. Acknowledge: server confirms the assignment
DHCP provides: IP address, subnet mask, default gateway, DNS server, lease time.

notes.title: File Transfer & Remote Access Protocols
notes.body:
FTP (File Transfer Protocol): transfers files between client and server. Port 20 (data), 21 (control). Unencrypted — passwords sent in plaintext. Now largely replaced by SFTP/FTPS.

SFTP (SSH File Transfer Protocol): FTP over SSH tunnel — fully encrypted. Port 22. Use this instead of FTP.

FTPS: FTP + TLS/SSL encryption. Ports 990/989. Different from SFTP.

SSH (Secure Shell): encrypted remote login and command execution. Port 22. Replaced Telnet. Supports key-based authentication (more secure than passwords). Used by system administrators to manage servers remotely.

Telnet: remote login protocol. Port 23. Completely unencrypted — transmits everything (including passwords) as plaintext. Should NEVER be used — replaced by SSH.

RDP (Remote Desktop Protocol): Microsoft's protocol for graphical remote desktop access. Port 3389. Used to remotely control a Windows computer with full GUI.

SNMP (Simple Network Management Protocol): monitors and manages network devices (routers, switches, servers). Port 161/162. Network administrators use SNMP to check device status, traffic statistics, and receive alerts.

topics.summary: HTTP (port 80) is stateless and request-response; HTTPS (port 443) adds TLS encryption. Email uses SMTP to send (port 25/587), POP3 to download (port 110), IMAP to sync across devices (port 143). DNS translates domains to IPs on port 53; DHCP auto-assigns network config via DORA. SSH (port 22) encrypts remote access, replacing Telnet (port 23, insecure). SFTP replaces FTP for encrypted file transfer.

sumCards.label: HTTP/HTTPS
sumCards.content:
HTTP: port 80, unencrypted · HTTPS: port 443, TLS
Methods: GET, POST, PUT, DELETE, PATCH

sumCards.label: Email Protocols
sumCards.content:
SMTP: send (25/587) · POP3: download (110, deletes)
IMAP: sync all devices (143, stays on server)

sumCards.label: DNS Records
sumCards.content:
A: IPv4 · AAAA: IPv6 · MX: mail
CNAME: alias · NS: nameserver · TXT: text

sumCards.label: Remote Access
sumCards.content:
SSH: encrypted remote (port 22) — USE THIS
Telnet: unencrypted (port 23) — NEVER USE
RDP: Windows GUI remote (port 3389)

qa.q: Explain the difference between HTTP and HTTPS. Why should all websites use HTTPS?
qa.a: HTTP (port 80) transmits data in plaintext — anyone on the same network can intercept and read data using Wireshark. HTTPS (port 443) adds TLS encryption — all data is encrypted between browser and server, preventing eavesdropping and tampering. All websites should use HTTPS because: passwords and credit card numbers are protected, modern browsers warn users about HTTP sites as 'Not Secure', and search engines (Google) rank HTTPS sites higher.

qa.q: Compare POP3 and IMAP. Which would you recommend for a professional who uses email on multiple devices?
qa.a: POP3 downloads emails to the client device and typically deletes them from the server. Emails only exist on one device — no synchronisation. IMAP leaves emails on the server and syncs across all devices — read/delete/move on phone reflects on laptop. For a professional using a phone, laptop, and tablet, IMAP is the correct choice because changes on any device are reflected everywhere.

qa.q: What is the DHCP DORA process? What information does DHCP provide to a client?
qa.a: DORA: 1) Discover — client broadcasts 'I need an IP address'. 2) Offer — DHCP server offers an available IP. 3) Request — client accepts and formally requests that IP. 4) Acknowledge — server confirms the assignment. DHCP provides: IP address, subnet mask, default gateway (router), DNS server address(es), and lease time (how long the assignment is valid).

qa.q: Why should Telnet never be used and what should replace it?
qa.a: Telnet transmits all data — including usernames, passwords, and commands — in plaintext. Anyone on the same network can capture a Telnet session with Wireshark and read every keystroke. SSH (Secure Shell, port 22) must replace Telnet because: it encrypts the entire session, supports public-key authentication (more secure than passwords), and provides the same remote access capabilities securely.

qa.q: List the DNS record types and explain the purpose of each.
qa.a: A: maps hostname to IPv4 address (www.site.com → 203.0.113.5). AAAA: maps hostname to IPv6 address. CNAME: canonical name alias — maps one domain to another (mail.company.com → company.com). MX: mail exchanger — directs email for the domain to the correct mail server. NS: nameserver — identifies which DNS server is authoritative for the domain. TXT: text — used for domain verification, SPF email validation, DKIM. PTR: reverse DNS — maps an IP address back to a hostname.

topics.subtitle: 1.1 Definition & History · 1.2 Characteristics · 1.3 Deployment Models · 1.4 Benefits & Risks

notes.title: What is Cloud Computing?
notes.body:
Cloud computing is a technology that provides computing resources (servers, storage, databases, networking, software) over the internet. Users access data and applications on remote servers instead of local hardware.

The 'Cloud' refers to servers accessed over the internet plus the software and databases on them. Computing is any activity using computers to manage, process, and communicate information.

NIST definition: a model for enabling ubiquitous, convenient, on-demand network access to a shared pool of configurable computing resources that can be rapidly provisioned and released with minimal management effort.

notes.title: Evolution of Cloud Computing
notes.body:
• 1950s–70s (Early Computing): Large mainframe computers; multiple users shared via time-sharing — the foundation of shared computing resources.
  • 1980s–90s (Networking & Virtualisation): PCs and LANs emerged; virtualisation allowed multiple VMs on one physical server.
  • 1990s–2000s (Grid & Distributed Computing): Grid computing allowed multiple computers in different locations to work together as one system.
  • Mid-2000s (Birth of Cloud): AWS launched EC2 and S3 in 2006; IaaS, PaaS, SaaS models emerged; businesses began shifting to cloud-based solutions.
  • 2010s–Present (Modern Cloud): Google Cloud, Azure, IBM Cloud expanded; AI, Big Data, IoT integrated; edge computing and hybrid cloud models emerged.
  • Future: Serverless computing, quantum computing, enhanced security, more multi-cloud strategies.

notes.title: Five Essential Characteristics (NIST)
notes.body:
1. On-demand self-service: provision resources without human interaction from the provider. Example: create a VM on AWS instantly.
  2. Broad network access: accessible via standard mechanisms from any device (laptop, phone, tablet).
  3. Resource pooling: multi-tenant model — provider serves multiple customers from the same infrastructure, dynamically assigned based on demand.
  4. Rapid elasticity: scale in/out quickly; resources appear unlimited. Example: add servers during peak traffic, remove after.
  5. Measured service: metered like a utility — pay only for what you use (storage consumed, CPU hours, bandwidth).

notes.title: Deployment Models
notes.body:
• Public Cloud: owned by provider (AWS, Azure, GCP), shared infrastructure, lowest cost. Best for general workloads.
  • Private Cloud: dedicated to one organisation — higher control and security, higher cost. Best for sensitive data and regulatory compliance.
  • Community Cloud: shared by organisations with common concerns (e.g., healthcare, government). Shared cost with sector-specific compliance.
  • Hybrid Cloud: mix of public + private — balance cost vs control. Run sensitive workloads on private, general workloads on public.

notes.title: Advantages & Disadvantages
notes.body:
Advantages:
  • Cost Reduction — no expensive hardware purchases (CapEx → OpEx)
  • Scalability — instantly scale up or down based on demand
  • Reliability — providers offer high uptime SLAs (99.9%+)
  • Instant software updates — provider manages patches
  • Improved performance — global infrastructure reduces latency
  • Universal access — access from any device, anywhere
Disadvantages:
  • Requires constant internet connection
  • Poor performance on slow internet connections
  • Vendor lock-in — migrating providers is complex
  • Data sovereignty — data may be stored in foreign jurisdictions
  • Multi-tenancy security concerns

topics.summary: Cloud computing delivers on-demand computing resources over the internet on a pay-as-you-go model. It evolved from 1950s mainframes through virtualisation to AWS launching in 2006. NIST defines 5 characteristics: on-demand, broad access, pooled, elastic, measured. Deployment models: public (cheapest), private (most secure), community, hybrid.

sumCards.label: NIST 5 Characteristics
sumCards.content: On-demand · Broad access · Pooled resources · Rapid elasticity · Measured service

sumCards.label: Deployment Models
sumCards.content:
Public: shared, cheapest · Private: dedicated, secure
Community: sector-shared · Hybrid: mixed

sumCards.label: Key Advantages
sumCards.content: Cost reduction · Scalability · Global access · Auto-updates · Reliability

sumCards.label: Cloud Timeline
sumCards.content: 1950s mainframes → 1990s virtualisation → 2006 AWS → 2010s multi-cloud → Future: quantum

qa.q: Define cloud computing and explain what the 'cloud' refers to.
qa.a: Cloud computing is a technology that provides computing resources (servers, storage, software) over the internet on demand. The 'cloud' refers to remote servers accessed via the internet — users store and process data on these remote servers rather than local hardware. NIST defines it as on-demand network access to a shared pool of configurable resources.

qa.q: State and explain all five essential characteristics of cloud computing (NIST).
qa.a: 1) On-demand self-service — provision resources without provider interaction. 2) Broad network access — accessible from any device via the internet. 3) Resource pooling — multi-tenant infrastructure dynamically assigned based on need. 4) Rapid elasticity — scale quickly; appears unlimited. 5) Measured service — billed like a utility, pay only for what you use.

qa.q: Compare public and private cloud deployment models.
qa.a: Public cloud: infrastructure owned and shared by a provider (AWS). Low cost, globally available, no hardware maintenance — but less control and security. Private cloud: dedicated to one organisation. Higher control and security, meets compliance requirements — but higher cost and requires in-house expertise.

qa.q: A Zambian bank wants cloud benefits but must keep customer data inside Zambia. Which model suits them?
qa.a: A private cloud deployed on-premises or in a Zambian data centre. It provides cloud benefits (elasticity, self-service, scalability) while ensuring data sovereignty — all customer data remains within Zambia, satisfying local regulatory requirements.

qa.q: Explain rapid elasticity and give a real-world business example.
qa.a: Rapid elasticity means computing resources can quickly scale up or down to match demand. Example: an online store during Black Friday sales — traffic spikes 10x, auto-scaling provisions extra servers to handle the surge, then terminates them after the sale. The business only pays for the extra capacity during the peak period.

topics.subtitle: 2.1 IaaS · 2.2 PaaS · 2.3 SaaS · 2.4 Shared Responsibility Model

notes.title: IaaS – Infrastructure as a Service
notes.body:
Provider manages: physical hardware, network, virtualisation layer. Customer manages: OS, middleware, runtime, applications, data.

Examples: AWS EC2, Azure Virtual Machines, Google Compute Engine
Best for: teams who need full OS-level control, system administrators, custom infrastructure.

Analogy: renting an empty building — you furnish and manage everything inside.

notes.title: PaaS – Platform as a Service
notes.body:
Provider manages: infrastructure + OS + runtime + middleware. Customer manages: applications and data only.

Examples: AWS Elastic Beanstalk, Google App Engine, Heroku, Azure App Service
Best for: developers who want to deploy code without managing servers.

Analogy: renting a furnished office — you bring your work, not the furniture.

notes.title: SaaS – Software as a Service
notes.body:
Provider manages everything including the application. Customer only uses the software via a browser or client — no installation, no maintenance, no servers to manage.

Examples: Gmail, Microsoft 365, Salesforce, Zoom, Google Docs
Best for: end-user productivity applications.
Limitation: limited customisation; dependent on provider's uptime.

Analogy: staying in a hotel — everything is provided and managed for you.

notes.title: Shared Responsibility Model
notes.body:
Security responsibilities are divided between the cloud provider and the customer:

Provider is responsible for security OF the cloud: physical data centres, hardware, network infrastructure, hypervisor layer.

Customer is responsible for security IN the cloud:
  • IaaS: patch OS, secure apps, configure firewalls, manage identities
  • PaaS: secure application code and data
  • SaaS: manage user accounts, access controls, and data classification

topics.summary: IaaS gives max control — you manage from OS upward. PaaS abstracts infrastructure — you manage app and data only. SaaS is fully managed — you only use the application. The shared responsibility model defines the security boundary between provider and customer at each service level.

sumCards.label: IaaS
sumCards.content:
You manage: OS → Runtime → App → Data
Examples: EC2, Azure VM, GCE

sumCards.label: PaaS
sumCards.content:
You manage: App + Data only
Examples: Heroku, App Engine, Beanstalk

sumCards.label: SaaS
sumCards.content:
You manage: accounts & access only
Examples: Gmail, Salesforce, Office 365

sumCards.label: Shared Responsibility
sumCards.content:
Provider: security OF cloud (hardware/network)
Customer: security IN cloud (OS/app/data)

qa.q: A startup wants to deploy a web app without managing any servers. Which service model suits them?
qa.a: PaaS. The startup pushes their code to Heroku or Google App Engine. The provider handles OS, runtime, scaling, and patching. Developers only manage application code and data. This eliminates infrastructure management overhead.

qa.q: Explain the shared responsibility model for IaaS.
qa.a: In IaaS, the cloud provider secures physical data centres, hardware, network, and hypervisor. The customer is responsible from the OS upward: patching the OS, securing the application, encrypting data, configuring firewalls, and managing identity and access controls.

qa.q: What is serverless computing and which service model does it extend?
qa.a: Serverless (e.g., AWS Lambda) is an extreme form of PaaS where developers write small event-triggered functions — no servers to provision, configure, or scale. The cloud auto-scales from zero to thousands of executions. Billing is per-invocation, not for idle servers. Best for event-driven tasks.

qa.q: List two real examples of each: IaaS, PaaS, SaaS.
qa.a: IaaS: AWS EC2, Azure Virtual Machines. PaaS: Heroku, Google App Engine. SaaS: Microsoft 365, Salesforce.

qa.q: A company stores HR data in a SaaS platform. What security responsibilities does the customer still have?
qa.a: The customer remains responsible for: 1) Managing user accounts and ensuring terminated employees lose access. 2) Enabling MFA for all users. 3) Configuring data access controls (who can view salary data). 4) Reviewing the provider's compliance certifications (SOC 2, ISO 27001). 5) Classifying data sensitivity.

topics.subtitle: 3.1 What is Virtualisation · 3.2 Hypervisors (Type 1 & 2) · 3.3 VMs vs Containers · 3.4 Docker

notes.title: What is Virtualisation?
notes.body:
Virtualisation is the technology that allows multiple virtual computing environments (virtual machines) to run on a single physical machine. Instead of buying many physical computers, we create virtual machines inside one powerful server.

Benefits: better resource utilisation, cost efficiency, scalability, isolation, disaster recovery.

A Hypervisor (Virtual Machine Monitor/VMM) is the software layer that sits between the physical hardware and the virtual machines — it allocates CPU, memory, storage, and network resources to each VM.

notes.title: Type 1 vs Type 2 Hypervisors
notes.body:
Type 1 Hypervisor (Bare Metal)
Installed directly on physical hardware — no host OS needed. Direct hardware access = best performance.
Examples: VMware ESXi, Microsoft Hyper-V, KVM (Linux)
Used in: data centres, enterprise production environments.

Type 2 Hypervisor (Hosted)
Installed as an application on top of an existing host OS.
Examples: Oracle VirtualBox, VMware Workstation
Used for: testing, development, learning — easier to install but slower.

Real-Life Example: A bank wants to run payroll, customer database, online banking, and email on one server. Install a Type 1 hypervisor → create 4 VMs. If one crashes, others continue.

notes.title: VMs vs Containers
notes.body:
Containers share the host OS kernel — only the application and its libraries are packaged. Unlike VMs, they do NOT include a full OS.

FeatureVirtual MachinesContainers
OSEach VM has full OSShare host OS kernel
SizeLarge (GBs)Small (MBs)
Boot TimeMinutesSeconds
IsolationStrong (full OS)Process-level
PerformanceModerateHigh

Analogy: VM = renting a full apartment. Container = renting a room in a shared house.

notes.title: Docker
notes.body:
Docker is the most popular platform for creating and managing containers. It solves the 'it works on my machine' problem.

Components:
  • Docker Image: immutable blueprint — contains OS base layer, app code, and dependencies
  • Docker Container: a running instance of an image
  • Dockerfile: text file with build instructions (FROM, RUN, COPY, EXPOSE, CMD)
  • Docker Hub: online registry for sharing images
Netflix Example: Netflix uses Docker containers to run streaming services. When many users watch at night, containers automatically scale up. When traffic drops, containers scale down — saving costs.

Kubernetes (K8s): orchestrates containers at scale. Key concepts: Pod (group of containers), Deployment (manages replicas), Service (network endpoint). Self-healing: restarts failed pods automatically.

topics.summary: Virtualisation allows multiple VMs on one physical machine via a hypervisor. Type 1 (bare-metal) is used in production; Type 2 (hosted) for development. Containers share the host kernel — lighter, faster, more portable than VMs. Docker packages apps as images; Kubernetes orchestrates containers at scale.

sumCards.label: Hypervisor Types
sumCards.content:
Type 1: bare-metal (ESXi, KVM) — production
Type 2: hosted (VirtualBox) — development/learning

sumCards.label: VM vs Container
sumCards.content:
VM: full OS, GBs, minutes, strong isolation
Container: shared kernel, MBs, seconds, lightweight

sumCards.label: Docker Components
sumCards.content:
Image: blueprint · Container: running instance
Dockerfile: build script · Hub: registry

sumCards.label: Kubernetes
sumCards.content:
Pod · Deployment · Service · Self-healing
Orchestrates containers at scale

qa.q: What is virtualisation and what is the role of a hypervisor?
qa.a: Virtualisation allows multiple virtual computers (VMs) to run on one physical machine by abstracting the hardware. A hypervisor (VMM) is the software layer that manages VMs — it allocates CPU, memory, storage, and network resources to each VM and isolates them from each other.

qa.q: What is the main difference between a Type 1 and Type 2 hypervisor?
qa.a: Type 1 (bare-metal) runs directly on physical hardware without a host OS — used in production data centres (ESXi, KVM). Better performance. Type 2 (hosted) runs as an app on top of an existing OS (VirtualBox) — easier to set up, used for development and learning, but has more overhead.

qa.q: How do containers differ from virtual machines?
qa.a: VMs include a complete guest OS — fully isolated, GBs in size, take minutes to start, significant resource overhead. Containers share the host OS kernel and only package the application and its libraries — MBs in size, start in seconds, more efficient. VMs offer stronger isolation; containers are more portable and resource-efficient.

qa.q: What problem does Docker solve and what is a Dockerfile?
qa.a: Docker solves the 'it works on my machine' problem — an app packaged as a Docker image runs identically on any system. A Dockerfile is a text file with build instructions: FROM (base image), RUN (commands), COPY (files), EXPOSE (port), CMD (default command). Running 'docker build' creates an image from the Dockerfile.

qa.q: A university wants to host e-learning, student database, website, and email on one server. How does virtualisation help?
qa.a: Install a Type 1 hypervisor on one powerful server and create 4 VMs: VM1 (website), VM2 (student database), VM3 (e-learning), VM4 (email). Each VM is isolated — if the e-learning VM crashes, the others continue. Cost is reduced (one server instead of four). Snapshots allow quick recovery.

topics.subtitle: 4.1 Object Storage · 4.2 Block & File Storage · 4.3 Availability & Redundancy · 4.4 Scalability

notes.title: Object Storage
notes.body:
Object storage stores data as objects — each object contains: the data itself, metadata (information about the data), and a unique identifier (ID).

Structure: flat — no traditional folder hierarchy. Accessed via HTTP/HTTPS APIs (GET/PUT requests).
Characteristics: highly scalable, ideal for large unstructured data, cost-effective.
Examples: Amazon S3, Azure Blob Storage, Google Cloud Storage
Use Cases: backups and archives, video and image storage, big data analytics, static website hosting, cloud-native applications.

Amazon S3 offers storage classes: Standard (frequent access), Intelligent-Tiering (auto), Infrequent Access, Glacier (archive — retrieval takes minutes to hours).

notes.title: Block Storage
notes.body:
Block storage divides data into fixed-size blocks and stores them separately. Each block has a unique address and acts like a physical hard drive partition.

Characteristics: high performance, low latency, must be attached to a VM, requires a file system.
Examples: Amazon EBS (Elastic Block Store), Azure Disk Storage
Use Cases: databases (MySQL, PostgreSQL), virtual machine boot volumes, enterprise applications, transaction systems.

Key difference: block storage is the fastest option for random read/write — databases need this for ACID transactions.

notes.title: File Storage
notes.body:
File storage stores data in a hierarchical structure (folders and files) — the traditional file system approach that all users recognise.

Characteristics: shared access by multiple VMs simultaneously, NFS/SMB protocols, familiar interface.
Examples: Amazon EFS (Elastic File System), Azure Files
Use Cases: shared directories, content management systems, team collaborative projects.

FeatureObjectBlockFile
StructureFlatBlocksHierarchical
PerformanceModerateHighModerate
Best ForMedia, backupsDBs, VMsShared files
ScalabilityVery HighHighModerate

notes.title: Availability, Redundancy & Scalability
notes.body:
Availability: how accessible data is when needed. High availability = minimal downtime, 24/7 access, automatic failover. Cloud providers achieve this with multiple data centres, load balancing, and replication. Amazon S3 Standard: 99.99% availability.

Redundancy: storing multiple copies of data to prevent data loss.
  • Local Redundancy (LRS): copies within the same data centre
  • Zone Redundancy (ZRS): copies across different availability zones
  • Geographic Redundancy (GRS): copies across different geographic regions — highest protection
Benefits: data protection, disaster recovery, business continuity.

Scalability: ability to increase or decrease storage capacity as needed. Cloud supports elastic scaling and pay-as-you-go — no upfront hardware purchases.

topics.summary: Cloud offers 3 storage types: object (flat, scalable, S3/Blob — for media and backups), block (fast, low-latency, EBS — for databases and VMs), and file (hierarchical, shared, EFS — for team collaboration). Availability and redundancy are achieved through data replication across zones and regions. Storage scales elastically on demand.

sumCards.label: Object Storage
sumCards.content:
Flat · data+metadata+ID · HTTP API
AWS S3, Azure Blob · Best: media, backups

sumCards.label: Block Storage
sumCards.content:
Fixed blocks · unique address · attaches to VM
AWS EBS · Best: databases, transactions

sumCards.label: File Storage
sumCards.content:
Hierarchical · shared NFS/SMB access
AWS EFS · Best: team shared directories

sumCards.label: Redundancy Types
sumCards.content:
LRS: local · ZRS: zone · GRS: geographic
More zones = more protection

qa.q: Compare object, block, and file storage. When would you use each?
qa.a: Object (S3): flat namespace, HTTP API, for unstructured data (images, videos, backups) — massively scalable. Block (EBS): raw disk blocks, low-latency random access, for databases and VM boot disks — fastest. File (EFS): hierarchical folders, shared NFS access, for team collaboration and shared directories — familiar interface.

qa.q: Which storage type is best for a database and why?
qa.a: Block storage. It divides data into fixed-size blocks with unique addresses, behaves like a physical hard drive, offers high performance and low latency random read/write access. Databases need this for ACID transactions. AWS EBS (Elastic Block Store) is the standard choice.

qa.q: Explain redundancy in cloud storage and name three types.
qa.a: Redundancy means storing multiple copies of data to prevent loss. Types: 1) LRS (Local Redundancy) — copies within the same data centre. 2) ZRS (Zone Redundancy) — copies across different availability zones in the same region. 3) GRS (Geographic Redundancy) — copies across different regions. More geographic spread = higher protection against disasters.

qa.q: What is availability in cloud storage and how do providers achieve it?
qa.a: Availability is how reliably data can be accessed when needed. High availability means minimal downtime and 24/7 access. Providers achieve it through: multiple data centres with replication, load balancing to redirect traffic if one fails, automatic failover, and SLA guarantees (Amazon S3 Standard: 99.99% availability).

qa.q: What are S3 storage classes and when would you use Glacier?
qa.a: S3 storage classes tier data by access frequency to optimise cost: Standard (frequent access, highest cost), Intelligent-Tiering (auto-moves between tiers), Infrequent Access (lower cost, less frequent), Glacier (lowest cost, archive — retrieval takes minutes to hours). Use Glacier for: long-term archives, compliance data, backups rarely needed.

topics.subtitle: 5.1 Cloud Virtual Machines · 5.2 Load Balancing · 5.3 Auto-Scaling · 5.4 Serverless & FaaS

notes.title: Virtual Machines in the Cloud
notes.body:
A Virtual Machine (VM) is a software-based computer with its own OS, CPU, memory, storage, and applications — running inside a physical server in a cloud data centre.

Cloud providers (AWS, Azure, GCP) offer VMs that users rent over the internet, paying only for what they use.

Characteristics:
  • Isolation: each VM runs independently — one crash doesn't affect others
  • Scalability: VMs can be added or removed in minutes
  • Flexibility: different OSes (Windows/Linux) run on the same physical server
  • Cost efficiency: pay per hour or per second of use
Examples: AWS EC2, Azure VM, Google Compute Engine
Advantages: no hardware purchase, remote access, high reliability, instant provisioning
Disadvantages: requires internet, security configuration needed, shared infrastructure performance.

notes.title: Load Balancing
notes.body:
Load balancing distributes incoming network traffic across multiple servers so no single server becomes overloaded.

Without load balancing: one server handles all requests → overwhelmed → crashes.
With load balancing: a load balancer distributes requests across 5 servers → each handles 20% → system stays stable.

Types of load balancers:
  • Round Robin: sends each request to the next server in sequence
  • Least Connections: sends to the server with fewest active connections
  • IP Hash: same client always goes to the same server (session stickiness)
Real-World Example: Netflix and Amazon use load balancing to handle millions of simultaneous users. During Black Friday, load balancers distribute traffic across hundreds of servers.

notes.title: Auto-Scaling
notes.body:
Auto-scaling automatically increases or decreases computing resources based on real-time demand — no manual intervention needed.

How it works:
  • Traffic increases → auto-scaler detects high CPU/load → provisions new servers
  • Traffic decreases → auto-scaler detects idle servers → terminates excess servers
Types of Scaling:
  • Vertical Scaling (Scale Up): increase power of a single server (e.g., upgrade RAM from 8GB to 32GB, upgrade CPU). Limited by hardware maximum.
  • Horizontal Scaling (Scale Out): add more servers to share the workload. Cloud platforms primarily use this — no limit on how many servers you can add.
Example: e-commerce site during Black Friday — auto-scaling adds 50 servers at peak, removes them after, saving cost.

notes.title: Serverless Computing & FaaS
notes.body:
Serverless Computing: developers write and deploy code without managing any servers. The cloud provider automatically manages all infrastructure, scaling, and execution.

Developers only: write code → upload → cloud runs it when triggered.

Function as a Service (FaaS): run small code functions triggered by events.
Examples: AWS Lambda, Azure Functions, Google Cloud Functions

Event examples: file uploaded to S3 → trigger Lambda to resize image. API request arrives → trigger function to query database. Schedule (cron) → trigger function to send daily report.

FeatureVirtual MachinesServerless
Server managementUser managesProvider manages
Cost modelPay for running VMPay per execution
ScalingManual or autoAutomatic
Best forLong-running appsEvent-driven tasks

topics.summary: Cloud VMs are rented, software-based computers — scalable, flexible, pay-per-use. Load balancing distributes traffic across multiple servers using strategies like round-robin. Auto-scaling adds or removes servers automatically; horizontal scaling (adding servers) is the cloud standard. Serverless (FaaS) runs event-driven code with zero server management and pay-per-execution billing.

sumCards.label: Cloud VMs
sumCards.content:
Software computers rented over internet
EC2, Azure VM, GCE · Pay per hour/second

sumCards.label: Load Balancing
sumCards.content:
Round Robin · Least Connections · IP Hash
Prevents server overload

sumCards.label: Auto-Scaling
sumCards.content:
Vertical: upgrade 1 server (RAM/CPU)
Horizontal: add more servers (cloud standard)

sumCards.label: Serverless / FaaS
sumCards.content:
AWS Lambda · Azure Functions · GCP Functions
Pay per execution · Event-triggered · No server mgmt

qa.q: What is a cloud virtual machine and what are its advantages over physical servers?
qa.a: A cloud VM is a software-based computer with its own OS, CPU, memory, and storage, running in a cloud provider's data centre. Advantages: no hardware purchase needed, provision in minutes, pay only per hour/second of use, easy to scale up or down, remote access from anywhere, high reliability with provider-managed infrastructure.

qa.q: Explain load balancing with a concrete example.
qa.a: Load balancing distributes incoming requests across multiple servers to prevent any one server from being overwhelmed. Example: 10,000 users visit an online store simultaneously. Without load balancing, one server crashes. With a load balancer, requests are distributed across 5 servers (2,000 each). The load balancer uses round-robin or least-connections algorithms to route traffic.

qa.q: Distinguish between vertical and horizontal scaling.
qa.a: Vertical scaling (scale up): increases the power of a single server — more RAM, faster CPU, more storage. Limited by hardware maximums. Horizontal scaling (scale out): adds more servers to share the workload — no single point of failure, no upper limit. Cloud platforms primarily use horizontal scaling for its flexibility and resilience.

qa.q: Define serverless computing and explain FaaS with an example.
qa.a: Serverless computing lets developers run code without managing any servers — the cloud provider handles all infrastructure. FaaS (Function as a Service) runs small code functions triggered by events. Example: AWS Lambda — when a user uploads a photo to S3, a Lambda function automatically triggers, resizes the image, and stores the thumbnail. Billing is per-invocation, not for idle time.

qa.q: Compare Virtual Machines and Serverless computing. When would you choose each?
qa.a: VMs: you manage the OS, suitable for long-running stateful applications, databases, complex workloads. Pay for running VM time. Serverless: cloud manages everything, ideal for short event-driven tasks (image processing, notifications, API backends). Pay per execution only. Choose VMs for persistent, complex workloads; choose serverless for event-triggered, stateless, short-duration tasks.

topics.subtitle: 6.1 Virtual Private Cloud · 6.2 Subnets · 6.3 DNS & Domain Routing · 6.4 Content Delivery Networks

notes.title: Virtual Private Cloud (VPC)
notes.body:
A Virtual Private Cloud (VPC) is a private, isolated network created inside a public cloud environment. Even though the underlying infrastructure is shared, each organisation's VPC is logically separated — like having a private data centre in the cloud.

Features of a VPC:
  • IP Address Range: each VPC has its own CIDR block (e.g., 10.0.0.0/16)
  • Network Isolation: resources are separated from other cloud tenants
  • Security Controls: security groups and network ACLs protect resources
  • Internet Connectivity: optional — attach an Internet Gateway for public access
  • Peering: connect multiple VPCs privately
Example: A bank deploys its online banking system in a VPC. Customer transactions stay isolated from other cloud tenants. The database sits in a private subnet; only the web server in the public subnet can reach the internet.

notes.title: Subnets (Public vs Private)
notes.body:
A subnet is a smaller network segment within a VPC. Subnets organise resources and control internet access.

Public Subnet: has a route to an Internet Gateway — resources can communicate with and be reached from the internet.
Examples: web servers, load balancers, public APIs, bastion hosts.

Private Subnet: no direct internet route — resources can only be reached from within the VPC.
Examples: databases, application servers, internal microservices, caches.

Best Practice Architecture:
Internet → Load Balancer (public subnet) → App Server (private subnet) → Database (private subnet)

Databases should never be placed in public subnets — this would expose them directly to the internet.

notes.title: DNS and Domain Routing
notes.body:
DNS (Domain Name System): translates human-readable domain names into machine-readable IP addresses.
Example: www.google.com → 142.250.190.78

DNS Resolution Process:
  1. User types www.netflix.com in browser
  2. Browser checks its local cache
  3. Query sent to local DNS resolver (ISP or Google 8.8.8.8)
  4. Resolver queries root nameserver → .com TLD → Netflix's authoritative nameserver
  5. Authoritative nameserver returns the IP address
  6. Browser connects to that IP and loads the page
DNS Record Types: A (IPv4 address), AAAA (IPv6), MX (mail server), CNAME (alias), NS (nameserver), TXT (text/verification).

Domain Routing: cloud providers use DNS-based routing to send traffic to the nearest or healthiest server — improving speed and reliability.

notes.title: Content Delivery Networks (CDNs)
notes.body:
A CDN is a globally distributed network of edge servers that cache and deliver content from the location nearest to the user.

Without CDN: User in Zambia → Origin server in USA → High latency (150-300ms)
With CDN: User in Zambia → Edge server in South Africa → Low latency (10-30ms)

How CDNs work:
  1. First user requests content → CDN fetches from origin, caches at edge
  2. All subsequent users → served from edge cache (fast)
  3. Cache expires → CDN refetches from origin
Popular CDN Services: Amazon CloudFront, Azure CDN, Cloudflare, Akamai

Advantages:
  • Faster website loading — content from nearest server
  • Reduced latency — shorter physical distance
  • Improved reliability — if origin fails, edge cache serves content
  • Reduced origin load — edge handles most traffic
  • DDoS protection — CDNs absorb attack traffic
Real-life example: YouTube uses CDNs — when you watch a video, it streams from a regional edge server, not from Google's main data centre.

topics.summary: A VPC is an isolated private network inside public cloud with custom IP ranges and security controls. Subnets divide the VPC — public subnets face the internet, private subnets protect databases and internal services. DNS translates domain names to IPs through a hierarchical lookup process. CDNs cache content at edge servers worldwide, delivering it from the nearest location to reduce latency and improve performance.

sumCards.label: VPC
sumCards.content:
Isolated private network in public cloud
Features: IP range, isolation, security groups, Internet GW

sumCards.label: Subnets
sumCards.content:
Public: web servers, load balancers (internet-facing)
Private: databases, app servers (no internet)

sumCards.label: DNS Process
sumCards.content: Domain → cache → resolver → root → TLD → authoritative → IP

sumCards.label: CDN Benefits
sumCards.content:
Edge servers worldwide · Nearest server delivers content
Faster · Less latency · Reduced origin load · DDoS protection

qa.q: What is a Virtual Private Cloud (VPC) and why is it important for cloud security?
qa.a: A VPC is a private, isolated network within a public cloud — logically separated from other cloud tenants even though the underlying hardware is shared. It is important because it provides: network isolation (only your resources in your network), custom IP addressing, security groups and ACLs to control traffic, and optional internet access. It lets organisations apply network security policies just as in a physical data centre.

qa.q: Explain the difference between a public subnet and a private subnet.
qa.a: A public subnet has a route to an Internet Gateway — resources can be reached from the internet (e.g., web servers, load balancers). A private subnet has no direct internet route — resources are only accessible from within the VPC (e.g., databases, application servers). Best practice: put web servers in public subnets; put databases in private subnets to prevent direct internet exposure.

qa.q: Describe how DNS works step by step when a user visits www.netflix.com.
qa.a: 1) User types www.netflix.com. 2) Browser checks local cache — not found. 3) Query sent to DNS resolver (e.g., 8.8.8.8). 4) Resolver queries root nameserver → returns .com TLD address. 5) Resolver queries .com TLD → returns Netflix's authoritative nameserver. 6) Resolver queries Netflix's authoritative nameserver → returns IP address. 7) Resolver caches result and returns IP to browser. 8) Browser connects to that IP.

qa.q: What is a CDN and how does it improve performance for a user in Zambia accessing a US-based website?
qa.a: A CDN is a globally distributed network of edge servers that cache content near users. Without CDN: the Zambian user's request travels to a server in the USA (150-300ms latency). With CDN: the content is cached at an edge server in South Africa or nearby (10-30ms latency). The CDN fetches from the US origin only once; all subsequent requests hit the nearby edge cache, dramatically reducing load time.

qa.q: List four advantages of using a CDN.
qa.a: 1) Faster loading — content delivered from nearest edge server reduces physical distance and latency. 2) Improved reliability — if the origin server fails, the edge cache can still serve cached content. 3) Reduced origin load — edge servers handle most traffic, protecting the origin. 4) DDoS protection — CDN infrastructure absorbs large attack traffic volumes.

topics.subtitle: 7.1 Identity & Access Management · 7.2 Encryption & Data Protection · 7.3 GDPR · 7.4 HIPAA

notes.title: Identity and Access Management (IAM)
notes.body:
IAM is a framework of policies and technologies ensuring the right people have the right access to the right resources. It answers three key questions: Who are you? What can you access? What can you do with it?

Core Concepts:
  • Identification: claiming an identity (typing a username)
  • Authentication: proving your identity (password, fingerprint, OTP)
  • Authorization: determining what you are allowed to do (read/write/admin)
  • Multi-Factor Authentication (MFA): requires 2+ independent credentials — e.g., password + SMS code. Even if password is stolen, attacker cannot login without the second factor.
Principle of Least Privilege: grant only the minimum permissions required. A Lambda function reading S3 should have s3:GetObject on that bucket only — not s3:* on all buckets.

IAM Components (AWS): Users, Groups, Roles (for services/applications), Policies (JSON allow/deny rules). Never use the root account for daily operations.

notes.title: Encryption and Data Protection
notes.body:
Encryption converts plaintext into unreadable ciphertext using an algorithm and key. It is the cornerstone of data confidentiality.

Data at Rest: data stored on disk, USB, cloud storage. Encrypted so that if storage is stolen, data cannot be read.
Examples: BitLocker (Windows), FileVault (macOS), AWS KMS (Key Management Service), Azure Key Vault.

Data in Transit: data travelling across a network. Encrypted to prevent interception.
Examples: HTTPS (TLS 1.2+), WhatsApp end-to-end encryption, SSH.

Key Management:
  • Provider-managed keys: provider handles creation and rotation — simpler but less control
  • Customer-managed keys (CMK): customer creates, controls, and can revoke the key — required for compliance frameworks (PCI-DSS, HIPAA)
Data masking/tokenisation: replace real sensitive data with fake tokens in non-production environments — developers can test without seeing real customer data.

notes.title: GDPR (General Data Protection Regulation)
notes.body:
GDPR is a comprehensive data privacy law of the European Union (EU). It applies to any organisation that handles personal data of EU citizens, regardless of where the organisation is located.

Key Principles:
  • Consent: must get clear permission before collecting data
  • Right to Access: individuals can request all data held about them
  • Right to be Forgotten: individuals can request deletion of all their data
  • Data Minimisation: collect only what is necessary
  • Data Breach Notification: notify authorities within 72 hours of a breach
  • Data Residency: may need to keep EU citizens' data within the EU
Real-Life Example: Cookie consent pop-ups on websites are a direct result of GDPR. Companies are legally required to get your consent before tracking you.

Penalties: up to €20 million or 4% of global annual turnover — whichever is higher.

notes.title: HIPAA and Cloud Security Best Practices
notes.body:
HIPAA (Health Insurance Portability and Accountability Act): US law setting standards for protecting sensitive patient health information (PHI — Protected Health Information).
  • Privacy Rule: protects individually identifiable health information
  • Security Rule: technical and administrative safeguards for electronic PHI (ePHI)
Example: doctors cannot share patient info with employers. Lost laptops with patient data must be reported. All ePHI must be encrypted.

Cloud Security Best Practices:
  • Enable CloudTrail/Activity Logs — log all API calls for audit
  • Use Security Groups and Network ACLs to restrict traffic
  • Never hardcode credentials in code — use IAM roles or secrets managers
  • Enable GuardDuty/Microsoft Defender for threat detection
  • Databases in private subnets — never expose directly to internet
  • Regular access reviews — remove unused accounts and permissions

topics.summary: IAM controls access through identification, authentication, and authorization. MFA adds a critical second security layer. Encryption protects data at rest (KMS, BitLocker) and in transit (TLS/HTTPS). GDPR governs EU citizen data with rights to consent, access, and erasure plus 72-hour breach notification. HIPAA protects US healthcare data. Apply least privilege, enable audit logging, use private subnets for databases.

sumCards.label: IAM Core Concepts
sumCards.content:
Identification → Authentication → Authorization
MFA: password + phone code · Least Privilege

sumCards.label: Encryption
sumCards.content:
At rest: BitLocker, AWS KMS (AES-256)
In transit: HTTPS/TLS 1.2+ · CMK for compliance

sumCards.label: GDPR Principles
sumCards.content:
Consent · Access · Erasure · Minimisation
72hr breach notification · €20M penalties

sumCards.label: HIPAA + Best Practices
sumCards.content:
PHI protected · ePHI encrypted · No public DBs
CloudTrail · Security Groups · GuardDuty

qa.q: Distinguish between authentication and authorization. Give an example of each.
qa.a: Authentication proves you are who you claim to be — e.g., entering your password or fingerprint at login. Authorization determines what you are allowed to do after authentication — e.g., a logged-in doctor can edit patient records, while a logged-in receptionist can only view contact information. Authentication comes first; authorization follows.

qa.q: What is MFA and why is it more secure than a password alone?
qa.a: Multi-Factor Authentication requires two or more independent credentials — e.g., something you know (password) plus something you have (phone with OTP app). Even if an attacker steals your password through phishing, they still cannot access your account without the second factor. This dramatically reduces the risk of account compromise.

qa.q: Explain the difference between encryption at rest and encryption in transit.
qa.a: Encryption at rest protects stored data — if a hard drive or USB is stolen, the thief cannot read the data without the decryption key (e.g., BitLocker on a laptop). Encryption in transit protects data as it travels across a network — prevents eavesdroppers from intercepting it (e.g., HTTPS encrypts all data between your browser and a website, protecting passwords and credit card numbers).

qa.q: What is GDPR and what are three rights it gives individuals?
qa.a: GDPR (General Data Protection Regulation) is a comprehensive EU data privacy law applying to any organisation handling EU citizens' data. Three individual rights: 1) Right to Access — request a copy of all data a company holds on you. 2) Right to be Forgotten — request deletion of all your personal data. 3) Right to Consent — companies must get clear permission before collecting data.

qa.q: A Zambian online shop starts selling to European customers. What GDPR obligations must they meet?
qa.a: Even based in Zambia, GDPR applies because they handle EU citizens' data. Obligations: 1) Add a cookie consent pop-up — get explicit consent before tracking. 2) Publish a clear privacy policy explaining data collection. 3) Allow customers to request or delete their data. 4) Report data breaches to the relevant authority within 72 hours. 5) Ensure EU customer data may need to stay within EU (data residency). Penalties can reach €20 million.

topics.short: Major Cloud Platforms: AWS, Azure & GCP
topics.subtitle: 8.1 Amazon Web Services · 8.2 Microsoft Azure · 8.3 Google Cloud Platform · 8.4 Comparison

notes.title: Amazon Web Services (AWS)
notes.body:
AWS is the market leader in cloud computing, launched by Amazon in 2006. It offers 200+ services with the largest market share (~32%) and most mature ecosystem.

EC2 (Elastic Compute Cloud): virtual servers (instances) you rent by the hour. Choose OS, CPU, RAM, storage. Supports Auto Scaling and Elastic Load Balancing.
Pricing models: On-Demand (pay per hour), Spot (up to 90% discount, interruptible), Reserved (up to 75% discount for 1-3 year commitment).

S3 (Simple Storage Service): object storage for any data type. 99.999999999% (11 nines) durability — store 10 million objects for 10,000 years and expect to lose one. Data in 'buckets'. Netflix stores all its video content in S3.

Lambda: serverless — run code without servers. Triggered by events (S3 upload, API call, DB change). Pay per invocation and compute time. Supports Python, Node.js, Java, Go, .NET. Max execution: 15 minutes.

RDS (Relational Database Service): managed database supporting MySQL, PostgreSQL, Aurora, Oracle, SQL Server. Auto-backups, patching, Multi-AZ replication — all handled by AWS.

notes.title: Microsoft Azure
notes.body:
Azure is Microsoft's cloud platform, launched in 2010. Second-largest provider (~22% market share). Strong integration with Microsoft enterprise products: Windows Server, Active Directory, Office 365.

Azure Virtual Machines: equivalent to AWS EC2. Choice of Windows or Linux. Azure Hybrid Benefit: use existing Windows Server licences to reduce costs.

Azure Blob Storage: Microsoft's object storage (equivalent to S3). Access tiers: Hot (frequent access), Cool (infrequent), Archive (rare, lowest cost). Lifecycle policies auto-move older content to cheaper tiers.

Azure App Service: fully managed platform for web apps and APIs. Deploy from GitHub or Azure DevOps. Supports .NET, Java, Node.js, Python, PHP. Auto-scales during traffic spikes. Built-in authentication.

Azure Active Directory (AAD): identity and access management — single sign-on (SSO) across Microsoft and third-party apps. Widely used in enterprises already using Microsoft 365.

notes.title: Google Cloud Platform (GCP)
notes.body:
GCP is Google's cloud platform, leveraging Google's expertise in large-scale computing, AI, and data analytics. Third-largest provider (~11% market share). Strengths: AI/ML, data analytics, Kubernetes (invented by Google).

Compute Engine: Google's IaaS virtual machines. Per-second billing after first minute. Preemptible VMs: up to 80% discount for interruptible workloads (batch processing). Live Migration: VMs continue running during host maintenance — no downtime.

Cloud Functions: serverless equivalent to AWS Lambda. Execution time up to 60 minutes (vs Lambda's 15). Triggers: HTTP, Cloud Storage, Pub/Sub, Firestore, scheduled. First 2 million invocations per month free.

BigQuery: GCP's fully managed data warehouse. Columnar storage, petabyte-scale SQL analytics. No infrastructure to manage. Pay per query. Google uses BigQuery internally for massive-scale data analysis.

GKE (Google Kubernetes Engine): managed Kubernetes — runs containers at scale. Google invented Kubernetes, so GKE is considered the gold standard for container orchestration.

qa.q: Name and briefly describe two core services offered by AWS.
qa.a: 1) EC2 (Elastic Compute Cloud): virtual servers rented by the hour. Choose OS, CPU, RAM, storage. Supports auto-scaling and load balancing. Pricing: On-Demand, Spot (90% discount, interruptible), Reserved (75% discount for commitments). 2) S3 (Simple Storage Service): object storage with 99.999999999% (11 nines) durability. Stores any data type in 'buckets'. Features: versioning, lifecycle policies, storage classes (Standard to Glacier), server-side encryption.

qa.q: Explain the difference between IaaS (EC2) and Serverless (Lambda).
qa.a: EC2 (IaaS): you get a virtual machine and manage everything from OS upward — patching, runtime, scaling config. Best for long-running, stateful applications needing full control. Lambda (Serverless): write code, define a trigger — AWS manages everything. Auto-scales from 0 to thousands instantly. Pay per invocation and compute time. Max 15 minutes. Best for short, event-triggered tasks.

qa.q: What makes Azure the preferred choice for enterprises already using Microsoft products?
qa.a: Azure integrates deeply with the Microsoft ecosystem: Azure Active Directory provides SSO across Microsoft 365, Teams, and thousands of third-party apps. Azure Hybrid Benefit allows using existing Windows Server and SQL Server licences on Azure, reducing costs. Familiar tools like PowerShell and Visual Studio integrate natively. Migration from on-premises Windows infrastructure is straightforward.

qa.q: What are spot/preemptible instances and when should you use them?
qa.a: Spot instances (AWS) / Preemptible VMs (GCP) are unused cloud capacity offered at massive discounts (up to 80-90%). Trade-off: they can be terminated with short notice (2 minutes AWS, 30 seconds GCP) when the provider needs the capacity back. Best for: batch processing, data analysis, machine learning training, rendering — workloads that are fault-tolerant and can resume from where they stopped.

qa.q: Compare the three major cloud platforms. What is each one's main strength?
qa.a: AWS: market leader (~32%) with 200+ services, most mature ecosystem, largest global infrastructure, biggest community — best for most general workloads. Azure: second (~22%), deepest Microsoft enterprise integration (Active Directory, Office 365, Windows Server) — best for organisations already using Microsoft. GCP: third (~11%), Google's expertise in AI/ML, invented Kubernetes so GKE is gold standard, BigQuery for petabyte analytics — best for data-heavy and AI-focused workloads.

topics.subtitle: 1.1 OOP Fundamentals · 1.2 Objects & Classes · 1.3 Encapsulation · 1.4 Java Features

notes.title: Fundamentals of Object Oriented Programming
notes.body: OOP organises software around objects — entities that combine data (fields) and behaviour (methods). The four pillars are: Encapsulation, Inheritance, Polymorphism, and Abstraction. OOP promotes code reuse, modularity, and maintainability over procedural programming.

notes.title: Objects and Classes
Data Abstraction and Encapsulation
notes.body: Abstraction: hiding complex implementation details, exposing only what is necessary (via abstract classes or interfaces). Encapsulation: bundling data and methods together and restricting direct access to internal state using access modifiers (private, protected, public). Data is accessed via getter/setter methods — also called data hiding.

notes.title: Java Platform Features
notes.body:
• Compiled and Interpreted: Java source (.java) compiles to bytecode (.class), then interpreted by the JVM
  • Platform Independent: 'Write Once, Run Anywhere' — JVM abstracts the OS
  • Object Oriented: everything is an object (except primitives)
  • Multithreaded: built-in support for concurrent programming
  • Distributed: built-in networking libraries (java.net)
  • High Performance: JIT compiler optimises bytecode at runtime

topics.summary: Java is a compiled, interpreted, platform-independent, object-oriented language. Classes are blueprints; objects are instances. Encapsulation bundles data and methods, hiding internal state. The four OOP pillars are Encapsulation, Inheritance, Polymorphism, and Abstraction.

sumCards.label: 4 OOP Pillars
sumCards.content: Encapsulation · Inheritance · Polymorphism · Abstraction

sumCards.label: Class vs Object
sumCards.content:
Class: blueprint/template
Object: runtime instance of a class

sumCards.label: Java Platform
sumCards.content:
Compiled → Bytecode → JVM → OS
'Write Once, Run Anywhere'

sumCards.label: Encapsulation
sumCards.content:
Private fields + public getters/setters
Hides internal state from outside

qa.q: What are the four pillars of Object Oriented Programming? Define each briefly.
qa.a: 1) Encapsulation — bundling data and methods, hiding internal state. 2) Inheritance — a subclass acquires properties of a superclass. 3) Polymorphism — one interface, multiple implementations. 4) Abstraction — hiding complex details, exposing only necessary interfaces.

qa.q: Explain the difference between a class and an object in Java with an example.
qa.a: A class is a blueprint that defines attributes and methods (e.g., class Dog { String name; void bark() {} }). An object is a specific instance created with 'new' (e.g., Dog d = new Dog(); d.name = "Rex"). Many objects can be created from one class.

qa.q: What does 'platform independent' mean for Java and how is it achieved?
qa.a: Platform independence means the same Java program runs on any OS without modification. Java achieves this by compiling source code to bytecode (.class files) rather than native machine code. The JVM (Java Virtual Machine), installed on each platform, interprets the bytecode — abstracting away OS differences.

qa.q: What is encapsulation and why is it important?
qa.a: Encapsulation bundles an object's data (fields) and behaviour (methods) together, and restricts direct access to the data using access modifiers (private). Data is accessed only via public getter/setter methods. This protects data integrity, makes code easier to maintain, and allows internal implementation changes without affecting external code.

qa.q: How does Java differ from a purely interpreted language like Python?
qa.a: Java is first compiled to platform-independent bytecode by the Java compiler (javac), then the JVM interprets or JIT-compiles the bytecode at runtime. This two-step process gives Java better performance than purely interpreted languages while maintaining platform independence. Python typically interprets source code directly, making it slower but simpler to run.

topics.subtitle: 2.1 Data Types · 2.2 Variables & Scope · 2.3 Type Casting · 2.4 Operators

notes.title: Variables and Data Types
notes.body: Java is statically typed — every variable must have a declared type. Primitive types: byte (8-bit), short (16-bit), int (32-bit), long (64-bit), float (32-bit), double (64-bit), char (16-bit Unicode), boolean (true/false). Reference types: String, arrays, objects. Constants: declared with final keyword — value cannot be changed after assignment.

notes.title: Scope of Variables
notes.body:
• Local variables: declared inside a method — only accessible within that method, not initialised by default
  • Instance variables (fields): declared in a class but outside methods — each object has its own copy, default-initialised (int→0, boolean→false, object→null)
  • Class variables (static): declared with static — shared across all instances, one copy per class

notes.title: Type Casting
notes.body:
Widening (implicit): smaller type automatically converts to larger type — no data loss. E.g., int x = 5; double d = x; (int → double).
Narrowing (explicit): larger type converted to smaller — may lose data, requires explicit cast. E.g., double d = 9.99; int x = (int) d; → x = 9 (decimal truncated).
Standard default values: int/long = 0, float/double = 0.0, char = '\u0000', boolean = false, object references = null.

notes.title: Operators and Expressions
notes.body:
• Arithmetic: +, -, *, /, % (modulus)
  • Relational: ==, !=, >, <, >=, <=
  • Logical: && (AND), || (OR), ! (NOT)
  • Assignment: =, +=, -=, *=, /=
  • Increment/Decrement: ++ (prefix/postfix), -- (prefix/postfix)
  • Conditional (Ternary): condition ? valueIfTrue : valueIfFalse
  • Bitwise: & (AND), | (OR), ^ (XOR), ~ (NOT), << (left shift), >> (right shift)
  • Special: instanceof (type check), . (member access), [] (array index)

topics.summary: Java is statically typed with 8 primitive types and reference types. Variables have local, instance, or class (static) scope. Widening cast is automatic; narrowing requires explicit cast. Java supports arithmetic, relational, logical, bitwise, and conditional operators.

sumCards.content: byte/short/int/long · float/double · char · boolean

Instance: per object
Static: per class
