qa.q: What is the difference between int and Integer in Java?
qa.a: 'int' is a primitive type — stored directly on the stack, no methods, no null. 'Integer' is a wrapper class — an object wrapping an int, can be null, has useful methods (Integer.parseInt(), Integer.MAX_VALUE). Java's autoboxing automatically converts between int and Integer when needed.

qa.q: What is the difference between prefix (++i) and postfix (i++) increment?
qa.a: Prefix (++i): increments i first, then returns the new value. Postfix (i++): returns the current value of i first, then increments. Example: if i=5, then j = ++i gives i=6, j=6; but j = i++ gives i=6, j=5.

qa.q: Explain widening and narrowing type casting with examples.
qa.a: Widening: int x = 5; double d = x; — automatic, no data loss (int fits inside double). Narrowing: double d = 9.99; int x = (int) d; — explicit cast required, x = 9 (decimal part is truncated, not rounded). Narrowing can cause data loss.

qa.q: What is a symbolic constant in Java and how is it declared?
qa.a: A symbolic constant is a named value that cannot be changed after initialisation. In Java, it is declared using 'final': final int MAX_SIZE = 100; By convention, constants are named in ALL_CAPS. Using 'static final' makes it a class-level constant shared by all instances.

qa.q: What does the modulus operator (%) do? Give an example.
qa.a: The modulus operator returns the remainder of integer division. Example: 17 % 5 = 2 (17 divided by 5 is 3 remainder 2). Common uses: checking if a number is even (n % 2 == 0), cycling through a range (index % arrayLength), extracting digits from a number.

topics.subtitle: 3.1 if/else Statements · 3.2 switch · 3.3 while/do-while · 3.4 for Loops

notes.title: Decision Making with if Statements
notes.body:
Simple if: executes a block only if condition is true.
if-else: two alternatives — if true, execute block A; otherwise block B.
else-if ladder: multiple conditions checked in sequence — only the first true branch executes.
if (score >= 90) grade = 'A';
else if (score >= 80) grade = 'B';
else if (score >= 70) grade = 'C';
else grade = 'F';

notes.title: The switch Statement
Looping Statements
notes.body:
while: checks condition before each iteration — loop body may never execute.
do-while: executes body first, then checks condition — body always executes at least once.
for: compact syntax — initialisation, condition, update in one line. Best when iteration count is known:
for (int i = 0; i < 10; i++) {
  System.out.println(i);
}

notes.title: Jumps in Loops & Labeled Loops
notes.body:
break: exits the innermost loop or switch immediately.
continue: skips the rest of the current iteration, jumps to the next.
Labeled loops: a label before a loop allows break label or continue label to control outer loops from inside nested loops:
outer:
for (int i = 0; i < 5; i++) {
  for (int j = 0; j < 5; j++) {
    if (j == 3) break outer; // exits both loops
  }
}

topics.summary: Java control flow includes if/else-if/else for conditions, switch for multi-branch matching, and while/do-while/for loops. break exits a loop; continue skips an iteration. Labeled loops allow breaking from nested loops.

sumCards.label: Decision Statements
sumCards.content: if · if-else · else-if ladder · switch (with break)

sumCards.label: Loop Types
sumCards.content: while: pre-check · do-while: post-check · for: compact/counted

sumCards.label: Loop Control
sumCards.content: break: exit loop · continue: skip iteration · labeled: control outer loop

sumCards.label: switch Syntax
sumCards.content: switch(expr) { case x: ...; break; default: ...; }

qa.q: What is the difference between a while loop and a do-while loop?
qa.a: A while loop checks the condition before the loop body executes — if the condition is false initially, the body never executes. A do-while loop executes the body first, then checks the condition — the body always executes at least once. Use do-while when the loop must run at least once (e.g., menu display).

qa.q: What happens if you forget the 'break' statement in a switch case?
qa.a: Without 'break', execution 'falls through' to the next case and continues executing all subsequent cases regardless of their condition. This is usually a bug but can be intentional (e.g., multiple cases sharing the same code). Always include 'break' unless fall-through is deliberate.

qa.q: Write a for loop to calculate the sum of numbers from 1 to 100.
qa.a: int sum = 0; for (int i = 1; i <= 100; i++) { sum += i; } System.out.println(sum); // Output: 5050. The loop initialises i=1, runs while i<=100, and increments i by 1 each iteration.

qa.q: When would you use a labeled break statement?
qa.a: A labeled break is needed when you want to exit from a nested loop structure from within the inner loop. Without a label, 'break' only exits the innermost loop. With a label, you can jump out of any enclosing loop. Example: searching a 2D array and breaking out of both loops once a value is found.

qa.q: What is the else-if ladder and how does it differ from multiple independent if statements?
qa.a: An else-if ladder tests conditions in sequence and executes only the first matching branch — once a true condition is found, the rest are skipped. Multiple independent if statements all get evaluated regardless. Use else-if when conditions are mutually exclusive; use separate ifs when multiple conditions can be true simultaneously.

topics.subtitle: 4.1 Defining Classes · 4.2 Constructors · 4.3 Method Overloading · 4.4 Static Members

notes.title: Defining a Class and Creating Objects
Constructors
Method Overloading
notes.body:
Method overloading allows multiple methods with the same name but different parameter lists (different number or types of parameters). The compiler selects the correct method at compile time based on arguments — this is compile-time polymorphism (static binding).
class Calculator {
  int add(int a, int b) { return a + b; }
  double add(double a, double b) { return a + b; } // overloaded
  int add(int a, int b, int c) { return a + b + c; } // overloaded
}

notes.title: Static Members
notes.body: Members declared with static belong to the class, not to individual objects. Accessed via class name (e.g., ClassName.staticField). Static fields are shared across all instances (e.g., a counter). Static methods cannot access instance fields or use this. main() is always static — the JVM calls it without creating an object.

topics.summary: Classes are blueprints with fields and methods. Constructors initialise new objects. Method overloading allows same method name with different parameters (compile-time polymorphism). Static members belong to the class and are shared across all instances.

sumCards.label: Class Structure
sumCards.content: Fields (state) + Methods (behaviour) + Constructors (initialise)

sumCards.label: Constructor Rules
sumCards.content: Same name as class · No return type · Called with 'new'

sumCards.label: Method Overloading
sumCards.content:
Same name, different parameters
Compile-time (static) polymorphism

sumCards.label: Static vs Instance
sumCards.content:
Static: belongs to class, shared
Instance: belongs to each object

qa.q: What is a constructor and how does it differ from a regular method?
qa.a: A constructor is a special method invoked automatically when an object is created with 'new'. Differences: 1) Same name as the class. 2) No return type (not even void). 3) Cannot be called explicitly like a regular method. 4) Used only to initialise the new object's state. Regular methods can be called multiple times at any point.

qa.q: What is method overloading and why is it useful?
qa.a: Method overloading allows defining multiple methods with the same name but different parameter lists (different types, number, or order). Useful because: it allows related operations to share a meaningful name (e.g., add(int, int) and add(double, double)), improving code readability without needing different names like addInts() and addDoubles().

qa.q: What is the difference between a static method and an instance method?
qa.a: An instance method is called on an object (student.display()) and has access to all instance fields and 'this'. A static method belongs to the class (Math.sqrt()), is called via class name, cannot access instance fields or use 'this', and can only directly access other static members.

qa.q: What happens if you don't define a constructor in a Java class?
qa.a: Java automatically provides a default no-argument constructor that initialises all fields to their default values (int→0, boolean→false, object→null). However, if you define any constructor with parameters, Java no longer provides the default constructor — you must explicitly define a no-arg constructor if you need one.

qa.q: Explain the 'this' keyword in Java.
qa.a: 'this' refers to the current object instance. Common uses: 1) Disambiguate between instance fields and constructor/method parameters with the same name: this.name = name; 2) Call another constructor from within the same class: this(name, 0); (constructor chaining). 3) Pass the current object as an argument to another method.

topics.subtitle: 5.1 Extending Classes · 5.2 Multi-level & Hierarchical · 5.3 Method Overriding · 5.4 Abstract & Final

notes.title: Inheritance: Extending a Class
Constructors in Inheritance
notes.body: When a subclass object is created, the superclass constructor is called first. If the superclass has a parameterised constructor, the subclass must call it explicitly using super(args) as the first statement in the subclass constructor. super refers to the immediate superclass.

notes.title: Method Overriding & Polymorphism
notes.body:
Method overriding: subclass provides its own implementation of a method already defined in the superclass — same method name, same parameter list, same return type. This enables runtime polymorphism (dynamic dispatch):
class Shape {
  void draw() { System.out.println("Drawing shape"); }
}
class Circle extends Shape {
  @Override
  void draw() { System.out.println("Drawing circle"); }
}
Shape s = new Circle();
s.draw(); // prints "Drawing circle" — dynamic binding

notes.title: Abstract Classes, Final & Dynamic Binding
notes.body: Abstract method: declared without a body — must be overridden by subclasses. Abstract class: cannot be instantiated; may have abstract and concrete methods. final variable: value cannot be changed (constant). final method: cannot be overridden. final class: cannot be subclassed (e.g., String is a final class). Dynamic binding: the JVM determines which overridden method to call at runtime based on the object's actual type.

topics.summary: Inheritance allows subclasses to reuse and extend superclass functionality. Method overriding enables runtime polymorphism — the correct method is selected at runtime based on actual object type (dynamic binding). Abstract classes define partial implementations; final prevents modification.

sumCards.label: Inheritance Types
sumCards.content:
Multilevel: A → B → C
Hierarchical: A → B and A → C

sumCards.label: Overriding Rules
sumCards.content:
Same name · Same params · Same return type
@Override annotation recommended

sumCards.label: Abstract Class
sumCards.content:
Cannot be instantiated
May have abstract + concrete methods
Subclass must implement abstract methods

sumCards.label: final Keyword
sumCards.content:
final variable: constant
final method: cannot override
final class: cannot extend

qa.q: What is the difference between method overloading and method overriding?
qa.a: Overloading: same method name with different parameters, within the same class. Resolved at compile time (static polymorphism). Overriding: subclass provides a new implementation of a superclass method with the exact same signature. Resolved at runtime (dynamic polymorphism). Overloading is about multiple methods in one class; overriding is about redefining in a subclass.

qa.q: What is dynamic binding and how does it work in Java?
qa.a: Dynamic binding (late binding) means the JVM decides which version of an overridden method to call at runtime, based on the actual type of the object — not the declared reference type. Example: Shape s = new Circle(); s.draw(); — the reference type is Shape, but the actual object is Circle, so Circle's draw() is called. This is the mechanism behind runtime polymorphism.

qa.q: What is an abstract class and how does it differ from a concrete class?
qa.a: An abstract class is declared with the 'abstract' keyword and cannot be instantiated directly — it must be subclassed. It can contain abstract methods (no body — subclasses must implement them) and concrete methods. A concrete class implements all methods and can be instantiated. Abstract classes are used to define a common template for subclasses.

qa.q: Explain multilevel and hierarchical inheritance with examples.
qa.a: Multilevel inheritance: a chain of inheritance — class C extends B, which extends A. Example: Animal → Mammal → Dog. Each level adds or specialises. Hierarchical inheritance: multiple subclasses extend the same superclass. Example: Animal → Dog, Animal → Cat, Animal → Bird. Multiple classes share the same parent.

qa.q: Why would you make a class 'final' in Java?
qa.a: Making a class final prevents it from being subclassed, ensuring its implementation cannot be altered by inheritance. Reasons: 1) Security — prevent subclasses from overriding security-critical methods. 2) Immutability — ensure the class's contract (e.g., String, Integer, wrapper classes). 3) Performance — JVM can apply optimisations when it knows there are no subclasses.

topics.subtitle: 6.1 Public · 6.2 Protected · 6.3 Private · 6.4 Default (Package) Access

notes.title: Access Modifiers Overview
notes.body:
Java uses access modifiers to control the visibility of classes, fields, and methods. Four levels exist:

public: accessible from anywhere — any class in any package.
protected: accessible within the same package AND by subclasses (even in different packages).
default (no modifier): accessible only within the same package — called package-private access.
private: accessible only within the same class — strongest restriction.

notes.title: Encapsulation with Private Fields
Protected Access
notes.body: protected members are accessible within the same package and by subclasses in other packages. This is the appropriate level for methods or fields that subclasses need to access but that should not be public API. In inheritance hierarchies, protected allows a parent class to share implementation details with its children while still hiding them from unrelated classes.

notes.title: Visibility Summary Table
notes.body:
ModifierSame ClassSame PackageSubclassOutside
private✓✗✗✗
default✓✓✗✗
protected✓✓✓✗
public✓✓✓✓

topics.summary: Java has four access levels: private (same class only), default/package (same package), protected (package + subclasses), and public (everywhere). Fields should be private with public getters/setters. Protected is used in inheritance for shared-but-not-public members.

sumCards.label: Private
sumCards.content:
Same class only
Best for fields (encapsulation)

sumCards.label: Default (no modifier)
sumCards.content:
Same package only
Package-private access

sumCards.label: Protected
sumCards.content:
Same package + subclasses
Used in inheritance hierarchies

sumCards.label: Public
sumCards.content:
Accessible from anywhere
Use for intended API/interface

qa.q: What is the default access level in Java and what does it mean?
qa.a: The default access level (package-private) is applied when no modifier is specified. The member is accessible only within the same package — other packages cannot access it, even subclasses in different packages. This is less restrictive than private but more restrictive than protected.

qa.q: Why should class fields generally be declared private?
qa.a: Declaring fields private enforces encapsulation — external code cannot directly read or modify the field. This: 1) Protects data integrity (you can validate input in setters). 2) Allows you to change the internal implementation without breaking external code. 3) Makes the class easier to maintain and debug.

qa.q: When would you use 'protected' instead of 'private'?
qa.a: Use 'protected' when a field or method should be private to the outside world but needs to be directly accessible by subclasses. Example: a superclass may have a protected 'logger' or internal helper method that subclasses should be able to use. If it were private, subclasses would have to use getters/setters instead of direct access.

qa.q: Can a subclass in a different package access a protected field of its superclass?
qa.a: Yes — protected access extends to subclasses in any package (not just the same package). The subclass accesses the protected member through inheritance (via 'this' or the inherited reference), but it cannot access it via a reference to another instance of the superclass outside the subclass context.

qa.q: What is the difference between 'private protected' and 'protected' access?
qa.a: 'private protected' is mentioned in older Java texts and some course outlines, but it is NOT a valid access modifier in modern Java. Java has exactly four access levels: private, default (no modifier), protected, and public. 'protected' already combines package access with subclass access. The concept was considered in early Java but not implemented.

Two-Dimensional Arrays
Strings in Java
notes.body: String is a class in Java (not a primitive). String objects are immutable — once created, their value cannot be changed. Useful methods: length(), charAt(i), substring(start, end), indexOf(str), toUpperCase(), toLowerCase(), equals(str) (NOT ==), concat(str), trim(). StringBuilder is used when frequent modification is needed (mutable, faster).

notes.title: Vectors & Wrapper Classes
notes.body:
Vector (java.util.Vector): a dynamic, resizable array — unlike arrays, Vectors grow automatically as elements are added. Thread-safe (synchronized). Common methods: add(), get(index), size(), remove().

Wrapper Classes: each primitive type has a corresponding class: Integer, Double, Float, Boolean, Character, etc. Allow primitives to be used as objects (e.g., in collections). Key methods: Integer.parseInt(), Integer.valueOf(), Double.parseDouble(). Autoboxing: automatic conversion from primitive to wrapper and back.

topics.summary: Arrays are fixed-size, same-type collections. 2D arrays are arrays of arrays. Strings are immutable objects — use StringBuilder for frequent modifications. Vector is a dynamically resizable, thread-safe array. Wrapper classes allow primitives to be used as objects with autoboxing.

qa.q: What is the difference between an array and a Vector in Java?
qa.a: Arrays: fixed size set at creation, faster, can hold primitives or objects, accessed by index. Vector (java.util.Vector): dynamic — grows automatically as elements are added, methods like add() and remove(), only holds objects (not primitives), thread-safe (synchronized). Use Vector (or ArrayList) when the number of elements is unknown or changes frequently.

qa.q: Why should you use equals() instead of == to compare Strings?
qa.a: == compares object references (memory addresses) — it checks if two variables point to the same object. For Strings, two String literals may or may not be the same object in memory due to the String pool. equals() compares the actual character content of the strings. Always use equals() for string comparison.

qa.q: What is autoboxing in Java and give an example?
qa.a: Autoboxing is Java's automatic conversion between primitive types and their corresponding wrapper classes. Example: Integer i = 42; — Java automatically boxes the int 42 into an Integer object. Unboxing is the reverse: int n = i; — Java automatically extracts the int from the Integer. This allows primitives to be used in collections (List<Integer>).

qa.q: How would you find the largest value in an int array?
qa.a: int[] arr = {5, 2, 8, 1, 9}; int max = arr[0]; for (int i = 1; i < arr.length; i++) { if (arr[i] > max) max = arr[i]; } System.out.println(max); // 9. Initialise max to the first element, then compare each subsequent element.

qa.q: Why is String immutable in Java and what are the implications?
qa.a: Strings are immutable for: 1) Thread safety — immutable objects can be shared across threads without synchronisation. 2) Security — String is used for class names, file paths, network connections — immutability prevents tampering. 3) String pool optimisation — JVM can reuse String literals. Implication: operations like concatenation (s + t) create new String objects. Use StringBuilder for repeated modifications to avoid performance issues.
